summary: Tight early‑Brent show with monster Scarlet>Fire and epic Sugaree
blurb: The band delivers a tight early‑Brent era performance highlighted by a monster Scarlet Begonias > Fire and an expansive 15‑minute Sugaree. Jerry and Phil trade dueling solos throughout, while Brent’s keyboards add upbeat energy. The second set flows smoothly into The Other One and a powerful close.
review: On May 5, 1979 the Grateful Dead took the stage at the Baltimore Civic Center with a fresh‑off‑the‑bench Brent Mydland already feeding the group new kinetic spark. From the opening “Jack Straw” through a soaring “Sugaree,” Garcia’s guitar was in fast‑noddling mode, trading blistering leads with Phil Lesh’s forward‑leaning bass – a partnership that only grew tighter on the legendary Scarlet Begonias > Fire transition. The second set built on that momentum: a punchy “Samson & Delilah” led into an impassioned “He’s Gone,” then into a dual‑drum “Space” before launching into a soaring, 15‑minute “The Other One.” Highlights include the epic Sugaree (where Brent makes his memorable entrance eleven minutes in) and a killer close with “Sugar Magnolia.” While a few recordings note minor tape quirks or softer vocal moments, the overall musical flow is tight, energetic, and quintessentially early‑Brent, making this show a must‑listen for fans of that era.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Four of six analyses describe the show positively (dueling solos, monster Scarlet>Fire, epic Sugaree) while two are mixed but contain no serious criticism. No consistent negative language appears, so a strong positive adjustment of +1.3 is applied.

best_recording.reason: Excellent early‑Brent soundboard capture with clear, well‑balanced mix; showcases dueling Garcia–Lesh solos and tight “Big Sugaree” despite minor track‑listing glitches.

key_highlights: Dueling Garcia‑Lesh solos throughout the set
Killer second‑set flow ending in a soaring The Other One

band_performance.Jerry: Fast‑noddling lead work, epic Wah‑Wah solo on Dancin', soaring guitar on Scarlet>Fire and a marathon Sugaree; overall strong despite occasional vocal strain.
band_performance.Phil: Engaged in dueling solos with Jerry, bass forward and “lets loose” during The Other One, all‑over the place on Scarlet>Fire but adds to the intensity.
band_performance.Bob: Vocals described as meek early then strained later, yet rhythm guitar remains solid throughout.
band_performance.Brent: Adds tighter, upbeat keyboard energy; memorable entrance 11 minutes into Sugaree marking the transition into his era.
band_performance.Mickey: Dual‑drum work provides steady foundation; Space segment shorter than usual but maintains groove.
band_performance.Billy: Provides reliable backbeat complementing Mickey; no major issues noted.
